past_medical_history: Previously treated for presumed migraine without aura by primary care physician without improvement over 4 months of triptan and NSAID therapy
Occasional tension-type headaches since adolescence, mild and self-limited

medications.frequency: As needed (used approximately 2-3 times per week without relief)

medications.indication: Nausea and vomiting associated with headache

family_history: Father with epilepsy diagnosed at age 30, on carbamazepine
Paternal aunt with colon cancer diagnosed at age 55
No family history of multiple sclerosis or autoimmune disease

social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children
social_history.exercise: Previously active, now limited by symptoms

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Memory intact for recent and remote events. Attention and concentration normal. No evidence of cognitive impairment on bedside testing.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. No relative afferent pupillary defect. Fundoscopic exam without papilledema or optic disc pallor. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and motor function symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk and tone in all extremities. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No pronator drift. No involuntary movements or tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in all extremities. No dermatomal sensory level identified.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+, triceps 2+, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally in upper extremities. Patellar 3+, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Bilateral Babinski signs present (extensor plantar responses bilaterally). No ankle clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. No dysmetria or intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait with intact tandem walking. Romberg sign negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign: Patient reports electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine and into all four extremities with neck flexion, described as brief paroxysms lasting 1-2 seconds. This was reproducible on examination.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive headache and electric shock-like sensations in all four limbs for 7 months, referred for evaluation of suspected brain mass.
patient.history_present_illness: A 37-year-old right-handed Saudi female elementary school teacher with no significant prior medical history presents for inpatient evaluation after being referred by her primary care physician for a suspected intracranial mass lesion. She reports a progressive frontal headache that began approximately 7 months ago, initially intermittent and mild but gradually worsening over the past 3 months to become near-constant and moderate in severity. The headache is described as a bilateral frontal pressure sensation, distinct from her previous occasional tension headaches, and is not associated with photophobia, phonophobia, visual aura, or postural changes. She has experienced frequent nausea and vomiting over the past 2-3 months, occurring approximately 3-4 times per week, occasionally triggered by bending forward or straining. Approximately 5 months ago, she began experiencing brief episodes of electric shock-like sensations radiating from her neck down into all four limbs, lasting only 1-2 seconds and occurring multiple times daily, particularly when she looks down at her desk while teaching. She denies any focal weakness, persistent numbness or tingling, visual changes, diplopia, hearing loss, difficulty swallowing, sphincter dysfunction, urinary incontinence, or bowel changes. She has not experienced any seizures. She reports mild fatigue over the past 3 months and has noticed some difficulty concentrating at work, which she attributes to the persistent headache and poor sleep from nausea. There has been no weight loss, night sweats, fever, rash, joint pain, or other systemic symptoms. She was previously started on sumatriptan and ibuprofen by her primary care physician for a presumed migraine diagnosis approximately 4 months ago, but has had no significant improvement. Her physician subsequently ordered outpatient imaging which raised concern for a possible brain lesion, prompting this referral for further evaluation.

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter adjacent to the body of the lateral ventricle

signal_characteristics.DWI: Peripheral restricted diffusion with open ring pattern
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: Open-ring enhancement with break in ring oriented toward cortical gray matter

findings.mass_effect: Mild perilesional edema without significant mass effect or midline shift
findings.borders: Partially well-defined with incomplete ring enhancement

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, frontal horn region
Subcortical white matter of right frontal lobe
Subcortical white matter of left parietal lobe, juxtacortical

additional_observations: Prominent intralesional venules noted within the largest right periventricular lesion, best seen on susceptibility-weighted imaging
Ventricles normal in size and configuration
Normal flow voids in the major intracranial vessels
mri.impression: Multiple supratentorial and infratentorial white matter lesions with a dominant 2.1 cm right periventricular lesion demonstrating open-ring enhancement, peripheral restricted diffusion, and prominent intralesional venules. The perilesional edema and ring enhancement raise concern for a mass lesion including high-grade glioma, metastatic disease, or cerebral abscess. However, the open-ring configuration with cortical-side break and prominent venules are atypical for neoplasm and could suggest a tumefactive demyelinating process. Additional smaller non-enhancing white matter lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial distributions are noted. Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive - 5 bands detected in CSF with no corresponding bands in paired serum sample (CSF-specific oligoclonal bands)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 18 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (normal: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 29 mg/dL (normal: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 137 mg/dL (serum glucose 219 mg/dL) (ratio 0.63 (normal: >0.6)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive - 5 bands detected in CSF with no corresponding bands in paired serum sample (CSF-specific oligoclonal bands). IgG index: 0.82 (elevated; normal: 0.28-0.66). IgG synthesis rate: 12.4 mg/day (elevated; normal: <8 mg/day). Cytology: No malignant cells identified. Flow cytometry: No abnormal lymphoid population detected. CSF culture: No growth at 72 hours. CSF AFB culture: No growth (final at 8 weeks). Myelin basic protein: 3.8 ng/mL (mildly elevated; normal: <1.5).

output.impression: Abnormal values: P100 latency - right eye 118 ms ms (H), P100 latency - left eye 121 ms ms (H).

additional_observations: Conus medullaris normal in position and signal
Mild degenerative disc changes at C5-C6, no significant stenosis
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with contrast. No evidence of spinal cord lesions or demyelinating plaques. Mild degenerative disc changes at C5-C6 are incidental and age-appropriate. Clinical correlation recommended.

CT Chest Abdomen Pelvis.value: No pulmonary masses, lymphadenopathy, or pleural effusion. Heart size normal. No hilar enlargement.
Liver, spleen, kidneys, adrenals, and pancreas normal. No masses or lymphadenopathy.
Normal pelvic organs. No masses or lymphadenopathy.
BI-RADS 1 - Negative. No suspicious masses, calcifications, or architectural distortion.

findings.region: Right periventricular white matter (2.1 cm enhancing lesion)
findings.signal: NAA_Cr_ratio: Mildly reduced (1.6; normal >2.0) suggesting mild neuronal injury; Cho_Cr_ratio: Mildly elevated (1.4; normal <1.3) suggesting increased membrane turnover; Lactate_peak: Small lactate doublet at 1.33 ppm present; Lipid_peak: No significant lipid peak; Glutamate_Glutamine: Mildly elevated
findings.description: MR spectroscopy of dominant right periventricular lesion

output.impression: MR spectroscopy of the dominant right periventricular lesion demonstrates mildly reduced NAA/Cr ratio and mildly elevated Cho/Cr ratio with a small lactate peak. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.query: tumefactive multiple sclerosis diagnosis MRI features open ring enhancement

results.title: Tumefactive demyelinating lesions: conventional and advanced MRI features
results.summary: Tumefactive MS lesions (>2 cm) can mimic brain tumors. Key distinguishing MRI features include: open-ring enhancement (specificity 84-96% for demyelination vs neoplasm), peripheral restricted diffusion, prominent intralesional venules on SWI, and the presence of additional typical MS lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, and infratentorial regions.

results.title: Differentiating tumefactive demyelination from glioma and other brain tumors
results.summary: Open-ring enhancement with the break oriented toward gray matter has high specificity for demyelination. In contrast, closed-ring enhancement is more typical of abscess and neoplasm. CSF oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index further support MS over neoplasm.

results.title: Diagnostic approach to tumefactive multiple sclerosis: avoiding unnecessary brain biopsy
results.summary: A systematic approach combining open-ring enhancement, VEP prolongation, CSF-specific oligoclonal bands, and MRI evidence of dissemination in space can often secure a diagnosis of tumefactive MS without biopsy. Stereotactic biopsy should be reserved for atypical cases with negative CSF and uncertain imaging.

output.summary: Tumefactive MS lesions (>2 cm) can closely mimic brain tumors on conventional MRI. The most reliable distinguishing features are: (1) open-ring enhancement with the incomplete portion oriented toward cortical gray matter, (2) prominent intralesional venules on SWI, (3) peripheral restricted diffusion rather than central restriction, (4) additional smaller lesions in typical MS distribution. CSF-specific oligoclonal bands and VEP prolongation provide strong supportive evidence. MR spectroscopy may show mildly reduced NAA and elevated choline but without the marked choline elevation or lipid peaks seen in high-grade glioma.

output.proposed: Interferon beta-1a (Avonex) 30 mcg IM weekly for relapsing-remitting MS
interactions: Sumatriptan: No significant interaction, but both may cause vasoconstriction; use with routine monitoring
contraindications: Decompensated liver disease (requires baseline and periodic liver function monitoring)
Known hypersensitivity to interferon beta or albumin
warnings: Monitor CBC with differential at baseline and periodically - may cause leukopenia
Monitor liver function tests at baseline, months 1, 3, 6 and periodically thereafter
Depression and suicidal ideation: monitor mental health status
Flu-like symptoms common in first months of treatment; pre-medication with ibuprofen or acetaminophen recommended
Monitor thyroid function at baseline and annually
output.formulary_status: Tier 3 specialty medication; prior authorization required
alternatives: Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone) 20 mg SC daily or 40 mg SC three times weekly
Dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera) 240 mg PO twice daily
For more aggressive disease: Natalizumab (Tysabri), Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus), or Fingolimod (Gilenya)

output.impression: Abnormal values: RNFL thickness - right eye (average) 82 um um (H), RNFL thickness - left eye (average) 79 um um (H), Ganglion cell layer - right eye 76 um um (H), Ganglion cell layer - left eye 74 um um (H).

differential.key_distinguishing: Closed-ring enhancement typically seen in glioma vs open-ring in MS; significant lipid peaks and markedly elevated choline on MRS; CSF cytology may show malignant cells; no oligoclonal bands; rapid progressive course without remission
Identifiable primary malignancy on systemic workup; typically at gray-white junction; CSF cytology may be positive; no oligoclonal bands; systemic imaging abnormal
Complete ring enhancement; central restricted diffusion (not peripheral); systemic signs of infection; elevated CSF WBC and protein; positive cultures; fever
Homogeneous enhancement; periventricular location but typically without satellite lesions in classic MS distribution; elevated CSF protein; abnormal flow cytometry; responds to steroids but relapses
Leptomeningeal enhancement; elevated serum ACE; hilar lymphadenopathy on chest CT; non-caseating granulomas on biopsy; different CSF profile

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium contrast to characterize the suspected mass lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple white matter lesions with a dominant 2.1 cm lesion showing open-ring enhancement, peripheral restricted diffusion, and intralesional venules

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, autoimmune markers, tumor markers, and infectious disease screening
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal autoimmune, infectious, and tumor markers; elevated serum glucose

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including cell count, protein, glucose, oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and flow cytometry
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (5 bands), elevated IgG index (0.82), elevated myelin basic protein, negative cytology and flow cytometry

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to assess for subclinical optic nerve demyelination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral P100 latency prolongation indicating subclinical optic nerve involvement

optimal_actions.action: Order NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4) and MOG-IgG antibody testing to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and mammography to exclude primary malignancy and metastatic disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal systemic imaging excluding metastatic disease and sarcoidosis

optimal_actions.action: Consider MR spectroscopy of the dominant lesion to further differentiate demyelination from neoplasm
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mildly reduced NAA, mildly elevated choline without lipid peaks - more compatible with demyelination than glioma

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature on tumefactive MS diagnostic features to guide interpretation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Open-ring enhancement with gray matter break, intralesional venules, and peripheral diffusion restriction are highly specific for tumefactive demyelination

optimal_actions.action: Request MRI cervical and thoracic spine to assess for spinal cord lesions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal spine MRI (no spinal cord lesions in this case)

optimal_actions.action: Synthesize findings: open-ring enhancement, CSF-specific oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index, bilateral VEP prolongation, negative systemic workup - diagnose tumefactive MS
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confident diagnosis of tumefactive multiple sclerosis without need for biopsy

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to characterize the lesion and note the open-ring enhancement pattern
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF oligoclonal bands and IgG index to identify intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis
Recognize open-ring enhancement with cortical-side break as characteristic of demyelination rather than neoplasm
Exclude metastatic disease with systemic imaging before diagnosing primary CNS pathology
Test NMO-IgG and MOG-IgG to exclude MS mimics
Recognize the significance of Lhermitte sign as a clinical clue pointing to demyelination
contraindicated_actions: Proceeding directly to stereotactic brain biopsy without completing non-invasive diagnostic workup (CSF analysis, VEP, NMO/MOG antibodies)
Initiating empiric chemotherapy or radiation therapy for presumed brain tumor without tissue diagnosis or complete workup
Starting high-dose corticosteroids before obtaining CSF analysis, as steroids may alter CSF findings
Dismissing the smaller satellite lesions as incidental and focusing only on the dominant mass-like lesion
Prescribing carbamazepine for the Lhermitte sign paroxysms without considering MS as the underlying cause
key_reasoning_points: The open-ring enhancement pattern with the break oriented toward cortical gray matter has high specificity (84-96%) for demyelinating disease and helps distinguish tumefactive MS from glioma or abscess
Prominent intralesional venules visible on SWI are characteristic of demyelinating lesions and are not typically seen in neoplasm
Peripheral restricted diffusion (leading edge of demyelination) differs from central restricted diffusion seen in abscess
CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (present in CSF but absent in serum) are found in >90% of MS patients and provide strong evidence for intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis
Bilateral VEP prolongation demonstrates subclinical dissemination of demyelination beyond the clinically apparent lesions, supporting dissemination in space criterion
The distribution of smaller lesions (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial) follows typical MS topography despite the atypical size of the dominant lesion
Normal systemic workup (CT body, tumor markers, infectious screening) and negative NMO/MOG antibodies help exclude important mimics
The elevated serum glucose (219 mg/dL) is a potential confounding finding but the CSF glucose ratio remains normal, and this likely represents stress hyperglycemia

Lhermitte sign (electric shock sensation with neck flexion) is a classic clinical sign of cervical cord or posterior column demyelination, providing a clinical clue that points away from an isolated brain mass

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle due to initial presentation mimicking a brain tumor. The dominant 2.1 cm enhancing lesion with perilesional edema triggers oncological concern. Key diagnostic clues (open-ring enhancement, intralesional venules, satellite demyelinating lesions, CSF oligoclonal bands, VEP prolongation) must be synthesized to arrive at the correct diagnosis of tumefactive MS rather than proceeding to biopsy or empiric oncological treatment.
